title: Supply- and Demand-Side Factors in Global Banking

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report (Number 818, June 2017) by Amiti, McGuire, and Weinstein decomposes international bank credit growth into common shocks, idiosyncratic supply shocks, and idiosyncratic demand shocks for the 2000-2016 period. The paper introduces the 'Anna Karenina Principle' to characterize how credit relationships behave differently during crises versus normal periods. During non-crisis periods, bank flows are driven by common global factors and local demand factors, but during crises, flows become highly sensitive to idiosyncratic supply shocks to a borrower country's creditor banks.
key_points: Study period covers 2000-2016, examining international banking flows and credit relationships

Authors use a decomposition method (from Amiti and Weinstein forthcoming) to separate growth in international bank credit into: common shocks, idiosyncratic supply shocks, and idiosyncratic demand shocks
The 'Anna Karenina Principle' states: all healthy credit relationships are alike, but each unhealthy credit relationship is unhealthy in its own way
During non-crisis years: bank flows explained by common global factor and local demand factor
During crisis periods: flows are affected by idiosyncratic supply shocks to borrower country's creditor banks
This framework helps explain why standard models break down during financial crises
JEL classification: F34 (International Lending and Debt Problems), G01 (Financial Crises), G21 (Financial Institutions and Services)
Full paper available only in PDF format (30 pages, 6.5 mb)
why_it_matters: This research provides a rigorous framework for understanding how international banking flows transmit financial shocks across borders. For policymakers and market participants, the key insight is that during crises, idiosyncratic bank-level supply shocks matter more than common factors—meaning the health of specific creditor banks to a borrowing country drives capital flow volatility, not just global conditions. This has implications for understanding contagion risk and designing macroprudential policy.
macro_context: This research supports understanding of international financial transmission mechanisms and crisis propagation through banking channels. The decomposition methodology remains relevant for analyzing how shocks to specific banking systems affect cross-border credit availability.
market_structure_context: The paper directly addresses market structure by analyzing the microstructure of international banking flows—specifically how the composition of credit relationships (common vs. idiosyncratic) changes between normal and stress periods. The finding that idiosyncratic supply shocks dominate during crises has direct implications for understanding banking market structure and lender-of-last-resort considerations.

Paper is approximately 8 years and 9 months old (June 2017), beyond the 90-day threshold—archival value claim must be assessed.
The 'Anna Karenina Principle' concept is referenced but full context of the theoretical framework is not verifiable from this source snippet.

notes: Historical research document (2017). This Staff Report from the NY Fed introduces important theoretical concepts about how international banking flows behave differently in crises vs. normal periods. The 'Anna Karenina Principle' and the decomposition methodology into common vs. idiosyncratic shocks provides conceptual framework for understanding banking market structure during stress. However, source content is limited to the abstract/crawl description; full paper is PDF-only (30 pages). Cannot extract detailed quantitative results, model parameters, or empirical estimates. Suitable as foundational research reference for understanding international banking market structure, but dated relative to current market conditions. Consider lower confidence for current market applications.
